text: While iron represents an ideal, earth-abundant alternative to noble metals in photoactive complexes, developing emissive and photochemically functional iron compounds remains a formidable challenge due to the rapid deactivation of their charge-transfer states. Consequently, emissive iron complexes are exceedingly rare, and dual emission has remained entirely elusive until now. In this work, we present the Fe(III) complex [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆] (where HImP = 1,1′-(1,3-phenylene)bis(3-methyl-1-imidazol-2-ylidene)), which exhibits unprecedented Janus-type dual emission originating from both ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. This unique photophysical profile is enabled by a ligand framework that integrates four N-heterocyclic carbenes with two cyclometalating aryl units. The low-lying π* orbitals of the cyclometalating architecture stabilize the MLCT states, preventing their relaxation into LMCT states. With a substantial lifetime of 4.6 ns, the highly potent MLCT-dominated state can drive both reductive and oxidative electron transfer reactions. These findings provide a new blueprint for light-harvesting iron complexes and establish a foundation for the use of iron in advanced photoredox catalysis.
